majorEmployers.name: Cleveland Area Hospital
majorEmployers.notes: The only hospital in Pawnee County, a Critical Access Hospital serving as a regional healthcare provider since 1963.

majorEmployers.name: Pawnee Public Schools
majorEmployers.notes: Public school district serving students throughout Pawnee County.

majorEmployers.name: Pawnee Nation of Oklahoma
majorEmployers.notes: Federally recognized tribe headquartered in Pawnee with jurisdiction in parts of Pawnee, Noble, and Payne counties.

majorEmployers.name: Pawnee Bill Ranch and Museum
majorEmployers.notes: 500-acre historic ranch and museum operated by the Oklahoma Historical Society.

uniqueFacts: On September 3, 2016, a magnitude 5.8 earthquake struck near Pawnee, making it the strongest earthquake ever recorded in Oklahoma history at that time.

The Pawnee County Courthouse, built in 1932, is listed on the National Register of Historic Places and features unique Art Deco architecture with carved bas-relief panels depicting Native American and pioneer scenes.
The Pawnee Bill Ranch and Museum, located in Pawnee, preserves the home of Gordon W. 'Pawnee Bill' Lillie, famous Wild West show entertainer, on 500 acres of the original 2,000-acre ranch.

The county was originally organized as 'Q County' following the Cherokee Outlet land opening on September 16, 1893, before being renamed Pawnee County in 1894 for the Pawnee tribe who had been relocated from Nebraska to the area between 1873 and 1875.
Chester Gould, creator of the Dick Tracy comic strip, was born in Pawnee County.
processServingNotes: The Pawnee Nation of Oklahoma maintains tribal jurisdiction in parts of Pawnee County, including on tribal trust lands. Process servers should coordinate with the Pawnee Nation Court at 918-762-3621 Ext. 141 for service involving tribal members or on tribal trust property.

The Pawnee County District Court is part of the 14th Judicial District, which includes both Pawnee and Tulsa counties. Case filings and record requests can be made through the Court Clerk's office at 500 Harrison, Room 300, Pawnee, OK 74058.
The county courthouse is located at 500 Harrison Street in Pawnee. Parking is available on the courthouse square. Office hours are Monday through Friday from 8 AM to 4:30 PM.
heroLead: Pawnee County in north-central Oklahoma is a 595-square-mile county steeped in Native American heritage, named for the Pawnee Nation whose tribal headquarters remain in the county seat of Pawnee. From the historic Pawnee Bill Ranch to the Cleveland Area Hospital serving as the county's only healthcare facility, Pawnee County blends Western history with rural Oklahoma charm. For reliable process serving throughout Pawnee County, call Just Legal Solutions at (539) 367-6832 or visit /contact.

faqs.question: Where is the Pawnee County Courthouse located and what are its hours?
faqs.answer: The Pawnee County Courthouse is located at 500 Harrison Street in Pawnee, Oklahoma 74058. The courthouse houses the Court Clerk's office on the third floor in Room 300. Office hours are Monday through Friday from 8:00 AM to 4:30 PM. The courthouse was built in 1932 and is listed on the National Register of Historic Places. For questions about case filings or records, call the Court Clerk at 918-762-2547 or reach Just Legal Solutions at (539) 367-6832.

faqs.question: Which judicial district is Pawnee County in and what does that mean for my case?
faqs.answer: Pawnee County is part of Oklahoma's 14th Judicial District, which also includes Tulsa County. District court cases filed in Pawnee County are heard at the Pawnee County Courthouse at 500 Harrison Street. District judges from the 14th Judicial District preside over cases, and at least one judge must reside in Pawnee County. The Court Clerk's office handles all filings, fee collections, and public records requests for the district court. Contact Just Legal Solutions at (539) 367-6832 for assistance with process serving in the 14th Judicial District.

faqs.question: Does Pawnee Nation tribal jurisdiction affect process serving in Pawnee County?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Pawnee Nation of Oklahoma maintains tribal jurisdiction in parts of Pawnee County, particularly on tribal trust lands and for enrolled tribal members. The Pawnee Nation's headquarters is in Pawnee, and their jurisdictional area includes parts of Pawnee, Noble, and Payne counties. If you need to serve process on a tribal member or on tribal trust property, you should coordinate with the Pawnee Nation Court. The tribal court clerk can be reached at 918-762-3621 Ext. 141. For assistance navigating tribal jurisdiction issues in Pawnee County, contact Just Legal Solutions.

faqs.question: What are the incorporated cities and towns in Pawnee County?
faqs.answer: Pawnee County includes two cities and eight towns. The cities are Cleveland (the largest community with approximately 3,200 residents) and Pawnee (the county seat with approximately 1,900 residents). The towns include Blackburn, Hallett, Jennings, Maramec, Ralston, Skedee, Terlton, and Westport. Each municipality may have its own municipal court for traffic and minor ordinance violations. For process serving anywhere in Pawnee County, including all cities and towns, call (539) 367-6832.

faqs.question: What major employers are in Pawnee County?
faqs.answer: Major employers in Pawnee County include Cleveland Area Hospital, the only hospital in the county located at 1401 West Pawnee Street in Cleveland with over 200 employees; Pawnee Public Schools with district offices at 615 Denver Street in Pawnee; and the Pawnee Nation of Oklahoma tribal government headquartered in Pawnee. The Pawnee Bill Ranch and Museum, operated by the Oklahoma Historical Society, is also a notable employer. Agriculture, oil and gas production, and healthcare are key sectors of the local economy. For process serving at any business in Pawnee County, contact Just Legal Solutions at (539) 367-6832.

faqs.question: What unique historical events have occurred in Pawnee County?
faqs.answer: Pawnee County was the epicenter of Oklahoma's strongest recorded earthquake, a magnitude 5.8 event that struck on September 3, 2016, near the city of Pawnee. The county was originally part of the Pawnee reservation before the Cherokee Outlet opening in 1893 brought non-Indian settlers. The county is named for the Pawnee Nation, whose tribal headquarters remain in Pawnee today. The historic Pawnee Bill Ranch, home to Wild West show entertainer Gordon W. 'Pawnee Bill' Lillie, is a major cultural attraction listed on the National Register of Historic Places. For process serving assistance anywhere in this historic county, call (539) 367-6832.
